name: Japan SIM Guide

disclosure.short: Disclosure: I work at Rakuten, and the guide includes an official employee referral link.
disclosure.medium: Disclosure first: I work at Rakuten, and the guide includes an official employee referral link. I call that out clearly on the page because I do not want to hide the incentive.
disclosure.long: Disclosure: I work at Rakuten and the guide contains an official employee referral link, so treat that as a visible conflict when judging the recommendation.

positioning.one_liner: A multilingual, newcomer-focused Japan SIM guide that answers the questions foreigners actually get stuck on.
positioning.soft_sell: The site is built to be useful first, compare honestly against docomo / au / SoftBank, and only point to the official Rakuten link if Rakuten still looks like the best fit.

bluesky.title: Japan SIM Guide
bluesky.description: Honest Rakuten vs docomo / au / SoftBank comparison, newcomer signup checklist, and monthly cost calculator in 11 languages.

proof_points: honest price comparison vs docomo / au / SoftBank
foreigner signup checklist covering residence card and payment friction
cost calculator for realistic monthly spend
11 language versions for newcomers and helpers
methodology page that makes the referral incentive visible

manual_targets.quora_question_seed: What is the best SIM card for foreigners moving to Japan in 2026?

launch.summary: Initial outreach sequence focused on earning the first trust-bearing backlinks and then reusing the same core story across article and Threads distribution.

tasks.note: Best first manual post for r/movingtojapan because it leads with usefulness instead of price.
Seed an exact-match answer for the core newcomer SIM question.
Canonical-first article draft for an early backlink and indexable distribution page.
Second article distribution channel once the publication ID is ready.
Fastest API-backed social launch because Bluesky only needs a handle plus App Password.
Use after tonight's Meta token setup to test the explicit-disclosure angle.
Optional follow-up if you want one non-English distribution test after the English post.

social-direct-week-2026-07-23.summary: Seven-day social-only plan for July 23, 2026 through July 29, 2026. The focus is direct traffic from Bluesky and Threads, not backlinks or article distribution.
social-direct-week-2026-07-23.goal: Ship the first cross-platform comparison posts, test the week-one friction angle in English, add one Spanish multilingual post, and finish the week knowing which angle to scale next.
prerequisites: Create one Bluesky App Password and keep BLUESKY_HANDLE plus BLUESKY_APP_PASSWORD ready in your shell.
Finish the Threads initial auth flow and keep THREADS_ACCESS_TOKEN ready before the first Threads slot.
rules: Do not post the exact same copy on Bluesky and Threads on the same day.
Keep at least six hours between same-day posts.
Cap the week at two posts per day, and stay English-first until the final multilingual test.
Preserve the source-tagged landing URLs so the site keeps the tailored entry flow.
social-direct-week-2026-07-23.review_prompt: By July 29, 2026, compare which angle earned the most useful traffic and reactions. Keep the winner, then repeat it next week with one English follow-up plus one multilingual support post.

tasks.note: First live post. Lead with the explicit comparison framing while the account is fresh.
Use the same angle on Threads later in the day, but keep the copy platform-native.
Test the week-one newcomer pain points on the platform with the lighter auth burden.
Bring the same practical-friction angle to Threads once the first comparison post has had time to breathe.
First multilingual test. Use Spanish to validate whether helper-focused copy travels better than pure price comparison.
Mirror the Spanish helper angle on Threads one day later so the platforms do not cannibalize each other.

arrival-friction.label: Week-one friction
arrival-friction.hook: Most Japan SIM advice is written for people who are already settled. Week one is the hard part.
arrival-friction.problem: New arrivals often do not have a Japanese credit card yet, may need eSIM immediately, and still need a realistic English-friendly signup flow.
arrival-friction.why_now: This angle works well when the post should feel helpful instead of promotional.
arrival-friction.cta: I turned that repeat answer into a public guide so people can compare first and decide faster.

honest-comparison.label: Honest Big 3 comparison
honest-comparison.hook: The usual Japan mobile discussion jumps from promo price to promo price and hides the real tradeoffs for foreigners.
honest-comparison.problem: People need to know the actual cost gap versus docomo / au / SoftBank and when Rakuten is not the right answer.
honest-comparison.why_now: This angle is useful for communities that are skeptical of referral links or sponsored-sounding posts.
bullets: real monthly cost gap vs the Big 3
honest-comparison.cta: The guide stays explicit about the incentive and still points out where Rakuten loses.

multilingual-helper.label: Multilingual helper resource
multilingual-helper.hook: A lot of SIM advice in Japan breaks down the moment the person helping is translating between languages.
multilingual-helper.problem: Friends, partners, and school staff often end up helping a newcomer choose a carrier, but the useful information is scattered and mostly Japanese or English only.
multilingual-helper.why_now: This angle works when the audience includes helpers, community admins, or people sharing resources with family.
bullets: same comparison and checklist across languages
useful for helpers sending a link to someone else
multilingual navigation with source-aware entry paths
multilingual-helper.cta: I wanted one link that a newcomer or helper could pass around without losing the practical details.
